Workforce Economics

Leverage Structures
Tomorrow

Three scenarios for how AI productivity gains will reshape consulting team sizes through 2035, modelled from a shared 16 FTE baseline.

Starting Point & Methodology

How the model is constructed

20
Pre-AI baseline FTE
per Partner
16
Near-term AI baseline
(BCG/Harvard 25% gain)
3×
Optimistic 2035 uplift
vs pre-AI baseline
All three scenarios branch from the 16 FTE shared departure point — the near-term AI effect (BCG/Harvard 25% productivity gain already priced in today). Additional productivity in each scenario is measured relative to this 2027 level, not the pre-AI baseline, to avoid double-counting. The "Total vs Pre-AI" column shows the full cumulative picture from 20 FTE.

Three Scenarios to 2035

Pessimistic, realistic, and optimistic trajectories for FTE per Partner

Slow AI adoption, limited workflow change, regulatory friction

Rapid AI capability growth, broad workflow automation, agentic AI handles most analytical tasks

2035 FTE 7
2035 add. productivity ~80% incremental

FTE per Partner Over Time

All three trajectories from 2024 baseline through 2035

Pessimistic Realistic (base case) Optimistic

Note on methodology: The "additional productivity" figures are always measured relative to the 2027 level (16 FTE) — not the pre-AI 20 FTE baseline — to avoid double-counting the near-term BCG/Harvard effect already embedded in the starting point. The "Total vs pre-AI" row captures the full cumulative picture. The realistic 2035 scenario is a clean 2× on pre-AI productivity; the optimistic 2035 is a 3×, compressing FTE per Partner to 7.

References

  • BCG / Harvard (~25% near-term gain). Fabrizio Dell'Acqua et al. (2023). Navigating the jagged technological frontier: Field experimental evidence of the effects of AI on knowledge worker productivity and quality. Harvard Business School Working Paper 24-013. hbs.edu
  • Merali (~39% total uplift by 2030 vs 2024 baseline). Merali, Z. (2024). The AI productivity paradox: Estimating long-run gains in knowledge work. Working paper cited in scenario modelling. The ~28% incremental gain shown in the realistic 2030 column nets out the 25% already captured at the 16 FTE baseline to avoid double-counting.
  • McKinsey Global Institute (30–50% analytical workstream compression). McKinsey & Company. (2023). The economic potential of generative AI: The next productivity frontier. McKinsey Global Institute. mckinsey.com
  • Optimistic scenario (3× by 2035, FTE per Partner → 7). Goldman Sachs. (2023). The potentially large effects of artificial intelligence on economic growth. Goldman Sachs Global Investment Research. These figures represent the upper bound of agentic AI automation of analytical and routine consulting tasks, consistent with Goldman Sachs' estimate of 25–50% task automation across professional services by the early 2030s.
  • Pessimistic scenario (regulatory friction, slow adoption). Tiwari, S. P. (2025). The implications of artificial intelligence in management consulting: A risk and barrier assessment. Multidisciplinary Reviews, 8, e2025244. https://doi.org/10.31893/multirev.2025244. Tiwari documents adoption barriers — regulatory risk, organisational resistance, and data governance constraints — that support a more conservative productivity trajectory in the near term.

AI in Consulting Operations

How AI is being embedded into day-to-day consulting workflows — from hiring to client delivery

BCG: AI Embedded in Consulting Workflows Operational AI

BCG uses AI across multiple touchpoints in its consulting operations — transforming how conversations are captured, how content is structured, and how outputs are delivered to clients.

Core Workflow
1
Conversation happens
Meeting, workshop, client session, or interview
2
AI transcribes & captures
Real-time or post-session transcription of everything said
3
AI structures the content
Organises raw transcript into themes, actions, and insights
4
AI generates outputs
Slide decks Summaries Insights Recommendations
Example Use Case — AI in Hiring Interviews
Step 1 — AI generates interview questions

Before the interview, GenAI creates role-specific, structured questions tailored to the candidate profile and competency framework.

Step 2 — AI transcribes during the interview

The conversation is captured in real time, creating a verbatim record without the interviewer needing to take notes.

Step 3 — Candidate dashboards generated by GenAI

From the transcription, GenAI automatically creates a structured dashboard with one "page" per candidate — surfacing key responses, competency signals, and comparative summaries for the hiring team.

Source: Marr, B. (2024, July 10). How BCG is revolutionizing consulting with AI: A case study. Forbes. forbes.com

Strategic Intelligence

AI's Disruption
of Consulting

How artificial intelligence is fundamentally restructuring the consulting industry's value chain — from workforce pyramids to client demand patterns.

The Reinforcing Cycle

A self-reinforcing disruption loop where each stage amplifies the next

The Four-Stage Disruption Loop

Customer AI adoption drives reduced consulting demand, which forces staffing cuts, which weakens firm competitiveness, which accelerates client self-reliance — a compounding cycle that is difficult to reverse once in motion.

From Scarcity to Overload

The fundamental shift in where consulting value is created

The Pyramid Shift

As GenAI lowers the barriers to generating strategy drafts, running analyses, benchmarking competitors, and building financial models (a trend noted in analyses by McKinsey,[8] Deloitte,[9] and the Stanford AI Index[10]), demand pressure for those basic consulting tasks decreases. At the same time, more companies can perform these routine functions internally (as observed in Business Insider reporting on Big Four and McKinsey workflows[11,12]), which increases demand for expert guidance and strategic judgment that only experienced consultants can provide (a theme emphasized by McKinsey[8] and BI interviews with consulting leaders[11,12]).

Scenario A — Compressed Pyramid

The base narrows but the hierarchy remains — fewer junior roles, with remaining staff distributed more evenly across levels as AI absorbs analytical groundwork.

Scenario B — Inverted Pyramid

The most disruptive outcome: junior roles collapse entirely as AI replaces them, leaving firms top-heavy with senior talent, partners, and directors driving value directly.

Two possible structural transformations of consulting firm staffing models driven by AI automation of junior-level work

The Pyramid Shift: From People Leverage to Capability Leverage

The traditional consulting pyramid — built on a wide base of junior analysts and associates performing data collection, research, and templated analysis — was designed for an era of information scarcity. As AI automates the bulk of these entry-level tasks (building decks, gathering market data, writing first-draft reports), consulting firms will employ significantly fewer junior staff. The inverted pyramid that emerges reflects a future where senior judgment, client relationships, and strategic interpretation carry most of the value — while AI handles the analytical groundwork that once required entire analyst cohorts. This is not simply downsizing: it is a structural rebalancing of the consulting workforce toward roles that remain genuinely difficult for machines to replicate.

Capabilities in AI-driven Consulting

The core skill sets required to deliver value in an AI-native consulting model

Recent research within AI, (Chinta, 2026; Rusthollkarhu et al., 2024; Myslicki, 2024; Scepanski et al., 2025; Zeng et al., 2025; Ponomareva et al., 2024; Islam et al., 2025; Xu & Cho, 2025; Tiwari, 2025; Tronnier et al., 2025), indicates that existing skill requirements will shift and new capabilities will emerge.

Consulting Firms
in the AI Era

Concrete examples of how consulting firms are acting on AI — through deployments, partnerships, and acquisitions.

Region

Examples in Practice — Nordics

What firms are actually doing in the Nordic region

Ekan Management 📍 Sweden 2 Initiatives
AI Strategy & Public Sector AI
AI Strategy📅 2024 · 📍 Sweden
AI x Strategi (with Eghed)

Together with AI boutique Eghed, Ekan created the "AI x Strategi" offering using their AIRA methodology to help Swedish organisations move from AI planning to real execution.[1]

Public Sector AI📅 2024 · 📍 Sweden
AI for Information Management

Ekan helped a Swedish public-sector organisation streamline document and case handling using an AI RAG model, enabling efficient classification and governance in line with Swedish archival law and GDPR.[2]

Signal: Specialist boutiques are productising AI strategy and deploying AI in regulated public-sector contexts — competing with larger firms on credibility and domain depth rather than brand.

BCG × Avinor 📅 2023–2024 📍 Norway AI Deployment
Optimizing Runway De-icing & Asset Maintenance

BCG worked with Avinor, Norway's state-owned airport operator, to build internal AI capabilities and deploy AI-supported systems for runway de-icing and asset maintenance, moving beyond advisory into hands-on tool deployment alongside client operations teams.

Signal: Strategy firms are expanding into AI delivery — embedding with client teams to build and integrate operational tools, blurring the line between consulting and implementation.[3]

Accenture 📍 Nordics 3 Initiatives
Sovereign AI · GenAI Deployment · M&A
Sovereign AI📅 2024 · 📍 Norway
Telenor AI Factory

Selected to jointly operate Telenor AI Factory's platform for sovereign and secure AI services, scaling high-performance AI compute and model-as-a-service for enterprise customers in Norway.[4]

GenAI📅 2024 · 📍 Sweden
Telia

Built a GenAI solution automating governance of a large telecom data repository, driving efficiency through metadata automation across tens of thousands of data fields.[5]

M&A📅 4 Mar 2025 · 📍 Denmark
Acquires Halfspace

Acquired Danish AI specialist Halfspace — 80 specialists, 100+ projects — to rapidly scale Nordic AI delivery capability and workflow automation.[6]

Signal: Accenture is building AI presence across the full stack in the Nordics — sovereign infrastructure, application deployment, and talent acquisition — cementing a broad, difficult-to-replicate position.

EY 📍 Sweden 2 Initiatives
AI Automation · AI Adoption Strategy
AI Automation📅 2023–2024 · 📍 Sweden
Insurance Claims Automation

EY deployed its Fabric Document Intelligence platform at a Nordic insurer, resulting in near real-time claims processing with 70% of documents correctly extracted automatically.[7]

AI Adoption📅 2024 · 📍 Sweden
Preem AI Strategy

EY facilitated an EY wavespace™ session for ~20 of Preem's senior leaders, generating 51 AI use cases for Sweden's largest fuel company, four selected for immediate PoC development.[8]

Signal: EY is combining operational AI automation with C-suite AI strategy facilitation — demonstrating a broad capability set spanning both technical delivery and organisational transformation.

Deloitte 📍 Sweden 2 Initiatives
Ecosystem Partnership · AI Observability
Ecosystem📅 2024 · 📍 Sweden
Partnership with AI Sweden

Deloitte partnered with AI Sweden to deepen AI collaboration in financial services and contribute to talent development across Sweden's AI ecosystem.[9]

AI Observability📅 9 Jul 2025 · 📍 Sweden
Dynatrace Partnership

Deloitte Sverige partnered with Dynatrace to deliver AI-driven observability for hybrid and multi-cloud environments, helping organisations meet ISO 27001 and DORA compliance.[10]

Signal: Deloitte is simultaneously embedding into national AI ecosystems and building AI infrastructure partnerships — a dual strategy combining long-term credibility with near-term technical delivery.

Implement Consulting Group × Semler Gruppen 📅 Autumn 2024 📍 Denmark GenAI Adoption
Rolling Out Generative AI Adoption Across 1,600 Employees

Implement ran a full generative AI adoption programme for Semler Gruppen — Denmark's largest automotive group — targeting 1,600 employees across geographically dispersed locations with vastly different AI skill levels, resulting in employees progressing from basic queries to complex AI-powered workflows.

Signal: Specialist change management firms are building a distinct AI adoption practice — demonstrating that successful AI transformation is as much about people and process as it is about technology.[11]

Examples in Practice — Global

AI-native challengers and global initiatives reshaping the consulting landscape

Accenture × ESPN
2024US
  • →ESPN is using generative AI to expand sports coverage to more fans and more sports.
  • →Accenture helped build a solution that generates recap summaries for Premier Lacrosse League and National Women's Soccer League games.
  • →The system uses ESPN and league-licensed data such as box scores, play-by-play, rosters, standings, schedules, and audio transcripts.
  • →ESPN editors review the AI-generated content before it is published on ESPN.com and the ESPN app.
  • →The main value is scalable fan engagement through faster content creation and broader coverage.

Source: accenture.com

Accenture × Best Buy
2024US
  • →Best Buy is using generative AI to improve customer and employee support experiences.
  • →A new AI-powered virtual assistant helps customers self-serve on BestBuy.com, in the app, or via customer support.
  • →Customers can use it for troubleshooting, delivery changes, scheduling, subscriptions, and membership management.
  • →Care agents also get gen AI tools that provide real-time recommendations, detect sentiment, and automate routine tasks.
  • →The goal is to make support more convenient, personalized, efficient, and empathetic.

Source: accenture.com

Deloitte × Global Automotive Leader
2024UK
  • →Deloitte worked with a global automotive leader to use GenAI to improve operational agility and customer value.
  • →The company had been slowed by manually reviewing thousands of complex supplier documents.
  • →That manual process affected procurement, production, and supply chain decision-making.
  • →Deloitte implemented a GenAI-driven strategy tailored to the client's operational needs.
  • →The case emphasizes GenAI as a shift toward greater agility, precision, and innovation in automotive operations.

Source: deloitte.com

Deloitte × Toyota
2025Japan / US
  • →Toyota is using agentic AI as part of its broader digital transformation.
  • →The company frames AI adoption around solving business problems, not chasing technology trends.
  • →One use case replaces a messy planning process that once relied on dozens of spreadsheets and many planners.
  • →Another use case improves vehicle ETA visibility and can automate communications with logistics providers and dealerships.
  • →Toyota says the real advantage comes from process redesign, employee upskilling, and building trust.

Source: deloitte.com

Examples in Practice — New Entrants

AI-native challengers entering the consulting landscape

Unity Advisory 📍 Global AI-Native Challenger
A PE-backed, AI-native "Big Four alternative" built to serve CFOs without audit constraints
  • Founded by ex-PwC and EY leaders, backed with ~$300M from private equity to scale fast
  • Focused on CFO advisory services (finance, tax, deals, transformation) for mid-market and PE-backed companies
  • Uses a partner-led, AI-driven model to deliver faster, more efficient outcomes
  • Leverages AI tools and automation while hiring senior-heavy teams (fewer juniors) to increase quality and speed — a live example of the inverted pyramid in practice
  • Competes with the Big Four via lean structure + performance-based / value pricing — no audit independence conflicts

Signal: Unity Advisory is the disruption mechanism made tangible — an AI-native firm purpose-built to take market share from incumbents by combining the inverted pyramid staffing model, AI-driven delivery, and outcome-based pricing from day one.

Eilla AI 📍 ● UK 📅 Founded 2022 AI-Powered Research Platform
An AI-powered research and workflow automation platform for financial professionals
  • Replaces hours of manual analyst work in M&A, VC, and private equity
  • Generates company profiles, market analysis, comparable valuations, and due diligence reports
  • Supports dealmaking tasks like buyer sourcing, market research, and investor materials
  • Automates repetitive workflows and produces reports, insights, and dashboards
Pricing & Proposition Evolution

Pricing
Strategies

How AI is fundamentally reshaping consulting economics — from pricing models and fee structures to the nature of the projects firms sell and deliver.

Development of Pricing in Consulting Projects (2021–2025)

Distribution of pricing models across professional services firms over the past five years, based on data gathered from Service Performance Insight's yearly Professional Services Maturity Benchmark reports (15th–19th editions, 2022–2026).

Sources: Service Performance Insight (2022–2026). Professional Services Maturity Benchmark, 15th–19th editions.

The Pricing Revolution

AI efficiency forces a structural rethink of consulting economics

Source: Tronnier, F., Löbner, S., Bernet, R., & Rannenberg, K. (2025). Consulting in the age of AI: A qualitative study on the impact of generative AI on management consultancy services. Proceedings of the Annual Hawaii International Conference on System Sciences. IEEE Computer Society.

The Central Argument

AI is not merely compressing the time it takes to do consulting work — it is destroying the logic of billing for that time in the first place. When a task that once required 40 analyst-hours can be completed in four, the billable-hours model stops being a neutral pricing mechanism and starts being a liability. Clients notice. Firms that cling to time-based billing risk being exposed as extracting rents on efficiency gains their clients are funding. The shift to outcome-based pricing is the industry's structural response to this exposure.[1][2][3][4]

1 — The Efficiency Trap

The traditional consulting model tied revenue to inputs: headcount multiplied by days multiplied by rate. AI severs that equation. McKinsey's enterprise AI tool Lilli — now used by 72% of its workforce — delivers up to 30% time savings on knowledge retrieval and synthesis alone.[2][3]

For a firm billing by the hour, productivity gains are self-defeating: the faster the work is done, the less revenue is recognised. Outcome-based pricing resolves this by decoupling revenue from effort — the firm is rewarded for the quality of the result, not the quantity of hours it took to produce it.[8][9]

2 — The Client Demand Signal

This is not a top-down strategic choice by firms — it is a bottom-up demand from clients. McKinsey's UK managing partner has reported that clients are increasingly bypassing traditional scoping conversations entirely: they arrive with the outcome they want and ask whether McKinsey can deliver it, tying compensation directly to results.[1][4][5]

This represents a fundamental reorientation of risk. In the old model, the client bore all execution risk — they paid regardless of whether the advice worked. Outcome-based structures transfer a share of that risk back to the advisor, aligning incentives with delivery.[6][9]

3 — The Market Signal

McKinsey has disclosed that roughly a quarter of its global fees are already generated through outcomes-based arrangements.[1][2][3][4][5] This is not a pilot programme — it is a disclosed commercial reality at the world's largest strategy firm. When a firm of that scale moves a quarter of its fee pool to a new pricing structure, it sets an industry benchmark that every competitor's procurement team will soon reference.

Slideworks' analysis of MBB pricing confirms that hourly billing, while common among independents (~29%), has never been the preferred model at the top tier — and that outcome-based and upside-sharing models are accelerating as the dominant direction.[10]

What This Means in Practice
Strategy work has already declined

Pure strategy now represents less than 20% of McKinsey's business. Clients are turning to consulting firms for deep implementation work across AI deployment, data infrastructure, and workforce transformation — domains where outcomes are measurable and accountability is concrete.[2][3][4]

EY signals a "service-as-software" future

EY leaders have publicly anticipated that AI may push consulting toward a "service-as-software" model where clients pay recurring fees for continuous access to intelligence and capability — rather than one-off project engagements.[2][7] This implies a fundamental shift in how consulting value is packaged and monetised.

Counter-Pressures & Transition Friction

The transition is not frictionless. Outcome-based pricing requires consultants to accept downside risk they have historically avoided — and demands far greater rigour in defining what "success" actually means before an engagement begins. Measuring outcomes in complex, multi-year transformations remains genuinely difficult, and disputes over whether agreed outcomes have been achieved introduce contractual and legal complexity absent from time-and-materials billing.[6][9]

There is also a revenue-recognition asymmetry: under billable-hours, firms receive payment throughout an engagement; under outcome-based structures, a portion of fees may be deferred until milestones are validated. For large firms accustomed to steady, predictable fee streams, this creates real cash-flow and forecasting challenges. The shift is directionally clear — but the pace of adoption will be shaped by how quickly firms solve these operational problems.[7][8][10]

Pricing Benchmarking

Real-world evidence of the pricing shift already underway at leading firms

McKinsey & Company 📅 Nov 2025 📍 Global Pricing Shift
From Billable Hours to Outcome-Based Fees
25%
of McKinsey's global fees now outcome-based
<20%
of McKinsey's business is now traditional strategy work
72%
of McKinsey staff are active users of Lilli, its AI tool

McKinsey has disclosed that a quarter of its global fees are now driven by outcomes-based pricing — a decisive break from the billable-hours model that has defined consulting economics for decades. Michael Birshan, McKinsey's UK managing partner, told reporters that clients are increasingly bypassing traditional scoping conversations entirely: rather than asking "what does this project cost?", they arrive with the outcome they want and tie compensation directly to whether McKinsey can deliver it. Some fixed cost components remain, but the direction of travel is clear.

This shift is directly enabled by AI. As McKinsey's internal tool Lilli compresses research and analysis time by up to 30%, the traditional justification for billing by the hour weakens — and clients are responding by demanding accountability for results rather than effort. Strategy work, once McKinsey's core offering, now represents less than 20% of the firm's business as clients turn instead to deep implementation support across data, AI, operations, and workforce transformation.

Signal: When the world's largest strategy firm publicly commits a quarter of its fee pool to outcome-based pricing, it sets a market benchmark that clients at every other firm will soon reference — accelerating the industry-wide shift away from billable hours.[1]

Globant — AI Pods 📅 5 Jun 2025 📍 Global Subscription Model
The First Monthly Subscription Model for AI-Powered Engineering

Globant AI Pods is the first monthly subscription model to access engineering, innovation, and creativity — no hours-based billing, no fixed-scope pricing. Rather than paying for human effort, clients subscribe to an AI Pod and receive token-based metered capacity, giving them full traceability and transparency on deliverables. The model mirrors how people consume streaming platforms: a predictable monthly fee for access to Globant's full AI-powered capability, delivered faster and at lower cost than traditional consultancy models.[2]

Outcome-Matched

Each AI Pod is designed to match the potential output of a high-performance team — work is measured in tokens used, not hours logged.

Faster to Market

Built to achieve faster time to market and greater productivity while making savings tangible — and Globant-verified to ensure top-quality results.

Agentic AI Core

Powered by Globant Enterprise AI (GEAI) — an AI-model-agnostic platform with agents for design, build, deploy, and orchestration of bespoke AI workflows.

Signal: Globant's AI Pods represent the most radical pricing departure yet seen in the industry — shifting from billable hours to a SaaS-style subscription anchored to outcomes. Bain describes it as "modular, scalable, and built for speed." If this model gains traction, it sets a new commercial benchmark that larger incumbents will be forced to respond to.[3]

Project Portfolio Transformation

Yesterday's flagship engagements versus tomorrow's high-value mandates

The New ERP: AI Transformation
Legacy Flagship

ERP Transformation

Multi-year SAP/Oracle implementations. Process standardization. Change management. Large junior analyst teams building data migration playbooks and testing scripts.

→
New Flagship

AI Transformation

Enterprise-wide AI readiness assessment, use-case prioritization, model deployment, governance frameworks, and workforce reskilling — the defining large-scale engagement of the 2020s.[3]

📊

Strategy-as-a-Subscription

Rather than one-off project engagements, firms offer continuous intelligence platforms — AI-powered dashboards providing clients with ongoing market monitoring, competitive signals, and scenario updates. BCG and McKinsey are piloting retainer models built on proprietary AI tools.[4]

🤖

AI Governance & Compliance

As regulators introduce AI-specific compliance requirements (EU AI Act, SEC AI disclosures), a new advisory category emerges: AI risk assessment, model auditing, and governance framework design. The Big 4 are particularly well-positioned given their existing compliance practices.[5]

⚡

Rapid Diagnostics

AI enables firms to compress what was once a 4-week discovery phase into a 72-hour AI-powered assessment. Bain's AI Radar and similar tools allow consultants to deliver board-ready diagnostics at a fraction of traditional timelines — enabling new lower-cost entry products that were previously uneconomical.[6]

🏗️

Co-Build & Product Development

Consulting is evolving from pure advisory to co-building AI products with clients. BCG X exemplifies this: strategy and delivery merge into a single engagement where consultants embed with client engineering teams to build and launch AI-native products. Fees shift from day rates to equity and success-based structures.[7]

🧠

Change Management & Workforce AI Adoption

Ironically, one of AI's largest new consulting categories is helping organizations adapt to AI itself. Workforce upskilling programs, AI change management playbooks, and culture transformation engagements are growing rapidly — particularly for large enterprises facing employee resistance to AI tooling.

📋

AI Due Diligence (M&A)

Private equity and M&A transactions now require AI capability assessment as part of commercial and technical due diligence. Consultants evaluate target companies' AI infrastructure, data assets, model inventories, and competitive moats — a new diligence workstream driven by AI's role as a core value driver in acquisitions.[8]

The Consulting Offer in Transition

Key dimensions shifting from traditional to AI-native models

Dimension Traditional Model AI-Native Model
Pricing model Daily rate × team size × weeks Outcome-based / subscription / success fee
Flagship engagement ERP transformation (2–3 years) AI transformation & governance (6–18 months)
Discovery phase 3–6 week research sprint 72-hour AI diagnostic
Deliverable PowerPoint recommendations deck Live AI tool / platform / co-built product
Team composition Heavy on junior analysts Lean senior team + AI toolchain
Client relationship Episodic project mandate Continuous intelligence partner
